coredata.dc:title: From cell membrane to endoplasmic reticulum: Tunable lipid membrane imaging by regulating the lipophilicity and internalization of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xing, Yanzhi
Li, Jiaxin
Leng, Han
Zhang, Yanfeng
E, Shuang
Wang, Jianhua
Chen, Xuwei
coredata.dc:description: The interactions between various organelles play a crucial role on cellular activity, and the design and construction of multi-organelle fluorescent probes is of great challenges. Herein, fluorescent CDs with tunable cell plasma membrane and lipid-rich organelle-targeting ability were fabricated via exquisitely regulating the ratio of tridecanedioic acid and m-diethylaminophenol as the co-precursor, combining the structure–activity relationship to modulate the CDs structure and surficial property. Condensation of m-diethylaminophenol in the presence of different amount of tridecanedioic acid was exploited to construct MCDs and ECDs for targeted cell membrane and endoplasmic reticulum imaging respectively. The appropriately incorporated alkyl chains as membrane anchoring group and the suppressed internalization by carboxyl group led to the retention of MCDs in cell membrane, while the excessive introduction of alkyl chains offered ECDs with high lipophilicity, resulting in the rapid internalization and subsequential distribution in endoplasmic reticulum membrane. The favorable membrane retention capability and excellent photostability make MCDs an excellent probe for long-term targeted-imaging of cell membrane in vitro, with remarkable tracking duration long to five passages and effectively display cell membrane morphological dynamics during cell division and cell death. Moreover, real-time evaluation of cholesterol consumption of Lo phase in cell membrane via ratiometric fluorescence imaging was achieved, attributed to the sensitive response of CDs to polarity variations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Organelle imaging
Cell membrane
Endoplasmic reticulum
Liquid ordered phase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical behaviors of MCDs and ECDs
Organelle-Targeting ability of MCDs and ECDs
Imaging of cholesterol-induced lipid order changes of biomembranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra (A) and Full scan XPS spectra (B) of CDs. High-resolution C 1 s spectra of MCDs (C) and ECDs (D).
TEM and HRTEM images (inset) of MCDs (A) and ECDs (B). XRD pattern of the two CDs (C).
Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells co-incubated with the two CDs and corresponding commercial organelle trackers.

Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells incubated with MCDs at different natural aging stages (A). Fluorescence images of cisplatin (25.0 mM) treated MCF-7 cells undergoing apoptotic cell death (inset: magnification images of cell membrane) (B). CLSM images of MCF-7 cells at different cell passages stained by MCD and DIL (C). Fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells incubated with MCDs during the process of cell division (D).
Fluorescence ratiometric images of MCF-7 cells stained with MCDs at different levels of cholesterol depletion.
The synthetic route and tunable localization of the two CDs (The amount of alkyl chains is inferred from XPS and NMR characterization).

head.ce:title: From cell membrane to endoplasmic reticulum: Tunable lipid membrane imaging by regulating the lipophilicity and internalization of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Sichuan Police College, Luzhou 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Sichuan Police College, Luzhou 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization of Ministry of Education, College of Life Sciences, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian 116600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization of Ministry of Education, College of Life Sciences, Dalian Minzu University, Dalian 116600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interactions between various organelles play a crucial role on cellular activity, and the design and construction of multi-organelle fluorescent probes is of great challenges. Herein, fluorescent CDs with tunable cell plasma membrane and lipid-rich organelle-targeting ability were fabricated via exquisitely regulating the ratio of tridecanedioic acid and m-diethylaminophenol as the co-precursor, combining the structure–activity relationship to modulate the CDs structure and surficial property. Condensation of m-diethylaminophenol in the presence of different amount of tridecanedioic acid was exploited to construct MCDs and ECDs for targeted cell membrane and endoplasmic reticulum imaging respectively. The appropriately incorporated alkyl chains as membrane anchoring group and the suppressed internalization by carboxyl group led to the retention of MCDs in cell membrane, while the excessive introduction of alkyl chains offered ECDs with high lipophilicity, resulting in the rapid internalization and subsequential distribution in endoplasmic reticulum membrane. The favorable membrane retention capability and excellent photostability make MCDs an excellent probe for long-term targeted-imaging of cell membrane in vitro, with remarkable tracking duration long to five passages and effectively display cell membrane morphological dynamics during cell division and cell death. Moreover, real-time evaluation of cholesterol consumption of Lo phase in cell membrane via ratiometric fluorescence imaging was achieved, attributed to the sensitive response of CDs to polarity variations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomembrane is a type of ubiquitous lipid structure that demarcates the cell surface and organelles, serving as a versatile platform for the implementation of various biochemical processes to provide enormous diversity for eukaryotic cells [1,2]. It is widely known that each compartment in cellular, like main communications artery, requires coordinating with each other to ensure the transportation in an orderly manner. Various cellular activities including ion and lipid exchange, as well as the regulation of the dynamics, localization, maturation and biogenesis of organelles, are mediated by membrane contact between membrane organelles [3], and the membrane processes have drawn great attention, e.g. dynamic membrane remodeling, nutrient transport and signal transduction [4–6]. Hence, the development of probes for membrane subcellular fraction is highly demanded to reveal membrane heterogeneity. Cell membrane is the typical membrane organelle locating in the periphery of cells, with a glycerophospholipid bilayer scaffold mainly composed of cholesterol, proteins and phospholipids. As barriers separating the internal and external cell volumes, it is essential for communication with the outer world [7,8]. However, probes for visualizing the membrane are not easily available, as most materials rapidly internalize inside the living cell. The cell membrane exhibits heterogeneity in lipid distribution both laterally and transversally, whose structure and function are controlled by its lipid order in a great measure [9–11]. Researches have demonstrated that the intense interaction between sphingomyelin (SM) and cholesterol (Chol) would lead to the formation of nano-domains with high lipid order. The formed highly packed state is named as liquid order (Lo) phase, distinguished from the slackly packed liquid disordered (Ld) phase formed by unsaturated lipids and Chol [12–14]. The lipid domain has received widespread attention due to its participation in many cellular functions, ranging from regulating membrane protein activity to membrane transportation, sorting and signal transduction. The complication raised from the characterization of the phase in cell membranes is related to the complex lipid composition, asymmetric distribution of lipids, the presence of membrane proteins and cytoskeleton [15,16]. Hence, the direct detection and visualization of lipid domain is of great challenging. Endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is the largest and most delicate complex membrane organelle in intracellular cavity, which is composed of continuous lipid bilayers of membranes and possesses a single continuous lumen, extending from the outer membrane of the nucleus to the cell membrane. ER organically connects various structures within the cell into a whole and carries metabolic pathways for the synthesis of cholesterol, phospholipids, steryl esters, triacylglycerols and other variety lipids [17,18]. However, fluorescent probes targeting ER are rarely reported, principally because the specific mechanism has not yet been clarified.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of reliable nanomaterial, are regarded as powerful fluorescence tools applied in subcellular visualizing due to their favorable fluorescence behaviors, low cytotoxicity and tunable chemical structure [19–21]. However, clear understanding of CDs for interactions with specific cell regions is not yet available, hindering the controllable targeting of CDs towards organelles. In recent years, by the aid of inherent nature or surface post modification, some examples of CDs have broken through the bottleneck. For instance, CDs with amino groups are found to be prone to aggregation in lysosomes for the specific localization, [22,23] triphenylphosphonium-functionalized CDs fulfill localization in mitochondria [24]. While these CDs are simply developed to label individual organelle and lack of the ability for specific localization of different organelles. Usually, the process of physiological changes is the joint consequence of various organelles, thus it is imperative to develop fluorescent probes capable to locate multiple organelles. In 2021, Li and co-workers exploited precursors with different amino substituents to modulate the surface chemical microenvironment of CDs, and achieved displaying fluorescence at various cellular regions [25]. This work revealed that the properties and targets of CDs are closely related to the precursors, laying the foundation to associate organelle targeting with carbon precursors. Subsequently, Chen et al proposed a precursor-governed strategy to fabricate four CDs possessing different surface groups and lipophilicity, which endowed them with different cell internalization pathways to achieve nucleus, lysosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi apparatus imaging [26]. Nevertheless, the properties of these CDs products were random due to the “blind box” type reaction of CDs. In addition, the separation process was complex and time-consuming. Despite the progress made in the preparation of multiple organelle imaging CDs, the reported literature failed to accomplish controllable targeting of various organelles by CDs from the source. Hence, a facile method to achieve tunable organelle targeting arbitrarily remains highly challenging.

In the present work, fluorescent CDs with tunable cell plasma membrane and lipid-rich organelle-targeting ability were designed and constructed using m-diethylaminophenol and tridecanedioic acid as precursors under controlled feeding ratio. Imaging of cell membrane and endoplasmic reticulum were achieved via the exquisite modulation of the surficial targetable moiety and the control of cell internalization of CDs. The polymerization/carbonization of m-diethylaminophenol formed a Nile Red (NR)-like structure with environment sensitivity, and produced two CD products, e.g., MCDs and ECDs, in the presence of different amounts of tridecanedioic acid. The low-amount introduction of tridecanedioic acid into CDs fabrication not only catalyze the polymerization process of m-diethylaminophenol, but also provide membrane anchoring moieties, e.g., alkyl chains and carboxyl, to suppressed the internalization of CDs, resulting in the retention of produced MCDs in cell membrane. Whereas, high-density alkyl chain on ECDs under excessive tridecanedioic acid introduction accelerated the CDs internalization, leading to distribution of ECDs in ER. Both MCDs and ECDs exhibited excellent photostability, superior fluorescence properties and favorable biocompatibility. MCDs was successfully used for targeting imaging of cell membrane in vitro and could be tracked as long as five passages, which was suitable for long-term cell imaging. Moreover, real-time evaluation of cholesterol consumption of Lo phase in cell membrane via ratiometric fluorescence imaging was achieved using MCDs as probe.

Tridecanedioic acid and m-diethylaminophenol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Amino acids, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, AlCl3, FeCl3·6H2O and FeCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliumbromide (MTT) assay kit was provided by Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). 1,4-dioxane, ethylacetate, tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, 2-deoxy-D-glucose, chlorpromazine, genistein, methyl beta-cyclodextrin and amiloride were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). ER-Tracker Red and DIL were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dioleoylphosphatidylcholine (DOPC), dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC), sphingomyelin (SM) and cholesterol were acquired from McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cells were provided by Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (resistivity > 18 MΩ·cm).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HTEM) was performed on a JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a VERTEX70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were obtained on an ESCALAB 250Xi analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA). Raman spectra were measured on an XploRA Raman spectrometer (Horiba, France). Photoluminescence behaviors were measured by a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–VIS) absorption spectra were acquired on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) under an optical path length of 1.0 cm. Cytotoxicity assay was conducted on a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader (Biotek Instruments, Inc, Germany). Cell images were taken on a FV-1200 confocal fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). Size distributions were performed on a Nano ZSE laser size analysis (Malvern, England).

In a typical synthesis procedure, different amounts of tridecanedioic acid (0.367 g or 0.734 g) and m-diethylaminophenol (0.248 g) with a molar ratio of 1:1 or 1:2 were dissolved in 20 ml of ethanol. Then the resultant mixture was sealed in a 50-mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After cooled to room temperature, the collected product was purified via dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da). The obtained solution was dried via vacuum condensation. Finally, CDs powder were dispersed in ethanol to obtain stock solution of 10 mg mL−1. The CDs synthesized with m-diethylaminophenol/tridecanedioic acid molar ratio of 1:1 and 1:2 were denoted as MCDs and ECDs, respectively.
For comparison, CCDs and ACDs were prepared via the above procedure using 0.248 g m-diethylaminophenol and 0.201 g malic acid or 0.556 g dodecylamine.

Large unilamellar vesicles were prepared as following. Lipid solution dissolved in chloroform was placed into a round neck flask, and directed to rotary evaporation under light-proof condition, and PBS buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4) was then added. The dissolved solution was extruded via a liposome biomembrane extruder to generate a suspension of multilamellar vesicles. The size of the filters was first 0.2 µm (7 passages) and thereafter 0.1 µm (10 passages). The monodisperse LUVs produced was measured using Nano ZSE (Malvern, England), with an average diameter of 0.11 µm. The phospholipid: cholesterol molar ratio in the case of mixed lipids including DOPC/Chol, DPPC/Chol and SM/Chol was 1:0.9. LUVs were labeled by the addition of 0.05 mg mL−1 ECDs and incubation for 5 min.

MCF-7 (Human breast cancer cells), HeLa (Human cervical cancer cells), A549 (Human non-small cell lung cancer cells) and MCF10A (Human normal mammary epithelial cell) were purchased from Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). MCF-7, HeLa and A549 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % FBS (inactivated fetal bovine serum) and 1 % antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin). MCF10A Cells were cultured in specific epithelial culture medium (CL-0525). All human tumor cell lines were cultured at 37 °C under of 95 % air/5% CO2 humidified atmosphere.

Cell viability was accessed by a typical MTT colorimetric assay using MCF-7 cells as the model. In detail, MCF-7 cells were seeded and cultured with 100 μL of fresh DMEM culture medium in 96-well micro-plates for 12 h. After removing the obsolete culture medium, cells were further incubated with 100 μL of serum-free culture medium containing CDs of various concentrations for 24 h. Thereafter the waste medium was replaced by fresh one, cells were treated with MTT (10 μL, 5 mg mL−1) for another 4 h followed by adding 100 μL of DMSO into each well. Finally, the 96-well plates were vibrated and the absorbance at 570 nm was recorded on a plate reader. The viability of cells was obtained by calculating the ratio of the absorbance of cells incubated with absence and presence of CDs.

MCF-7 Cells were detached by trypsin and seeded onto 20 mm petri dishes. After adherence, the cells were incubated with 500 μL of CDs (0.05 mg mL−1) for different time (MCDs 10 min, ECDs 30 min), and then washed with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) thoroughly. Subsequently, the MCF-7 cells were incubated with commercial organelle probes (DIL and ER-Tracker Red). Fluorescence imaging photographs of cells were subjected with a confocal fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan) using a 60 × oil immersion objective lens in sequential frame scanning mode. The CDs were excited using microscopy laser of 405 nm and recorded with a barrier filter BA 420–500, while the commercial Trackers were excited at 559 nm and recorded with a barrier filter BA 570–640 nm.

MCF-7 cells were pre-incubated with various pathway inhibitors at 37 ℃ for 40 min. After removing the inhibitor solutions, the cells were incubated with 0.05 mg mL−1 ECDs (500 μL) for 30 min. The fluorescence images were measured on a confocal fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan) with a 30 × objective lens (Ex/Em = 405/460–500 nm).

MCF-7 cells were used as model cells to study the cholesterol depletion of cell membrane. Adherent MCF-7 cells were pre-treated with MβCD (5 mM, prepared in PBS buffer of pH 7.4) for 30 min or 2 h. Then, 500 μL of MCDs (0.05 mg mL−1) were added and incubated for 10 min. The fluorescence images were captured using a 60 × objective lens by a confocal fluorescence microscope. The microscopy laser was set as follows: Ex/Em = 405/420–460 nm, 490–540 nm. For each pixel, the intensity was measured via Image J software for both channels, while pseudo-color scale was used for coding the ratio.

Solvatochromic fluorescent dye Nile Red (NR) is an efficient lipophilic probe exhibiting wide applications in characterizing the hydrophobic domains of model lipid membranes [27,28], while the rapid internalization rate prevents its application in cell membrane assay [29]. Hydrophobic fluorophores can be selectively retained on the plasma membrane via coupling with hydrophilic groups and long hydrophobic chains [30]. Therefore, fluorescent CDs was designed and synthesized for cell membrane imaging by using m-diethylaminophenol as precursor with tridecanedioic acid as catalyst in present study, and the CDs synthesis procedure was illustrated as Scheme 1 . The condensation of m-diethylaminophenol in the presence of tridecanedioic acid produced the fluorophore of NR-like structure through Friedel-Crafts reaction. The obtained fluorophore continued to polymerize to form carbon network. Subsequently, CDs with NR as the luminescent centers can be obtained in one-step through further carbonization. In the process, tridecanedioic acid not only acted as the catalyst to facilitate Friedel-Crafts reaction, but also served as a precursor to introduce the long alkyl chains to anchor the CDs in lipid bilayer and the hydrophilic carboxyl group to govern the internalization of CDs.

FT-IR spectra revealed that similar surface groups were comprised in the two CDs (Fig. 1 A). The characteristic stretching vibration of = C − O − C of diphenyl ether groups at 1043 cm−1 and C − N of aromatic tertiary amines at 1401 cm-1well suggested the polymerization of m-diethylaminophenol into the fluorophore of NR-like structure [31]. The stretching vibrations of O − H bonds led to a wide peak around 3428 cm−1, the C = O bonds was observed at 1641 cm−1, and the C = C/C = N bonds appeared at around 1480 cm−1. The bands centered around 2922 cm−1 and 2848 cm−1 represented the C − H and CH2 stretching frequencies for sp 3 carbon [32]. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR 1H) analysis further confirmed the presence of alkyl chains on the CDs and the higher methylene content in ECDs than MCDs (Fig. S1). The element identification certificated that there were carbon, oxygen and nitrogen existed in the two CDs, which located at 284 eV, 399 eV and 531 eV respectively (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1C–D) was well-fitted into sp2 carbon (284.2 eV), sp3 carbon (285.3 eV), nitrous carbon (C − O/C − N, 285.2 eV), and carboxyl carbon (COOH, 288.5 eV). The increase in the relative content of sp3 carbon and carboxyl carbon from MCDs to ECDs (Table S1) implied that more alkyl chains were incorporated in the final structure of CDs with the increase on the introduced tridecanedioic acid. The two peaks at 399.2 eV and 400.8 eV in the high resolution XPS of N 1 s for the two CDs (Fig. S2A) were assigned to C–N and N–H groups. The high resolution XPS results of O 1 s for the CDs (Fig. S2B) displayed two main peaks at 532.3 eV and 531.0 eV, which were attributed to C = O and C–OH/C–O–C groups. The chemical bonds deduced above, suggesting that the as-prepared CDs coincided well with the expected configuration.

TEM images illustrated in Fig. 2 A–B demonstrated that MCDs and ECDs were well dispersed near-spherical shape with average diameter of 3.52 ± 0.69 nm and 4.59 ± 0.64 nm, determined from 100 counts of random particles. HR-TEM images (Insets in Fig. 2A–B) provided that no crystal lattice was presented in MCDs and ECDs, indicating that these CDs products were virtually amorphous. The crystallinity of the two CDs was also supported by XRD patterns. As depicted in Fig. 2C, only broad peaks centered around 20.0° and 21.8° were observed for MCDs and ECDs, indicating their amorphous structure. Surface charge analysis indicated that both MCDs and ECDs were negatively charged, the Zeta potentials of MCDs and ECDs were −7.31 mV and −2.22 mV, respectively (Fig. S2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical behaviors of MCDs and ECDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–VIS spectra of MCDs and ECDs owned similar optical adsorption and tail elongation characteristics (Fig. S3A–B). Typically, the pronounced peak centered at 260 nm was attributed to the generation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores, e.g., n-π* transitions of the C = C/C = N. The peak at 303 nm could be assigned to n-π* transition of C = O bond. The band centered at 543 nm was originated from the π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bond conjugated sp2 hybrid from the carbon core of CDs [33,34]. Fig. S3C–D clearly showed that the CDs products exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors. The excitation/emission maximum of MCDs and ECDs were 390 nm/490 nm and 390 nm/485 nm respectively. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields of MCDs and ECDs were determined to be 14.8 % and 16.9 % respectively, and the average fluorescence lifetime derived from the fluorescence decay curves were 2.82 ns for MCDs (Fig. S3E) and 3.30 ns for ECDs (Fig. S3F).

Excellent anti-interference ability and fluorescence stability are quite imperative for carbon dots to be employed as probes for practical cell imaging. Within the pH values range of 5.0 to 11.0, there was almost no variation in the normalized fluorescence intensity of these two CDs, indicating that MCDs and ECDs were quite stable under different pH conditions (Fig. S4A–B). The existence of common coexisting substances (300 μM for ions and 100 μM for amino acids) in cells posed negligible interference on the fluorescence, demonstrating the excellent anti-interference ability of both CDs (Fig. S4C–D). The anti-photobleaching performance is key for long-term monitoring. No obvious fluctuation in fluorescence intensity was observed during the 2 h continuous irradiation process with 150 W xenon lamp (Fig. S4E–F). Consequently, both CDs possessed excellent photo-stability under different conditions and were considered as powerful candidates for sensing and fluorescence imaging in real biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organelle-Targeting ability of MCDs and ECDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability of CDs was evaluated via MTT assays (Fig. S5). Both CDs displayed low toxicity to cells and the survival rates of cells remained higher than 90 % even after treating with high concentrations of MCDs (300 μg mL−1) and ECDs (250 μg mL−1) for 24 h. The subcellular colocalization of CDs was investigated by co-staining of CDs and commercial organelle probes that the blue channel of MCDs overlapped well with the red channel of DIL (Fig. 3 ). The calculated Pearson’s correlation coefficient value was 0.92, demonstrating that MCDs precisely remained in cell membrane. In the case of ECDs, the emission distribution of CDs severely overlapped with that of ER-Tracker Red, accompanied by a Pearson’s correlation coefficient of 0.91. The result elucidated the superior ER-targeting ability of ECDs. The universal and specific distributions of both CDs were further demonstrated by incubating different cell lines including MCF10A cells, HeLa cells and A549 cells with CDs. As can be seen from Fig. S6 that regardless of cell type, MCDs and ECDs were able to stain cell membrane and endoplasmic reticulum with high Pearson’s correlation coefficient. These observations indicated the generalizable application of the two CDs in corresponding organelle imaging.

It has been demonstrated that CDs usually entered cells through endocytosis after interacting with cell membranes [26], The concrete internalization pathways and final intracellular localization of CDs are heavily dictated by their properties including surface chemistry, charge, size, hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity [35]. To understand the staining mechanism of ECDs, the internalization pathway was examined by incubating MCF-7 cells with CDs under different potent biochemical inhibitors including 2-DG (2-deoxyglucose, inhibit the energy-dependent pathway), AMI (amiloride, macropinocytosis inhibitor), CHP (chlorpromazine, clathrin-mediated endocytosis inhibitor), GEN (genistein, caveolae-mediated endocytosis inhibitor) and MβCD (Methyl-β-cyclodextrin, lipid raft-mediated endocytosis inhibitor). As illustrated in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity of ECDs in cells treated with 2-DG decreased to 28 %, manifesting that ECDs entered cells through energy-consuming endocytosis. The uptake rates of ECDs were significantly blocked in the presence of MβCD, but comparatively impervious by the other inhibitors, indicating that ECDs were mainly internalized by lipid raft endocytosis. It was reported that the internalization of weak anionic nanoparticles into cells mainly be ascribed to lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, facilitated by their lipophilicity [35,36]. The low Zeta potential of −2.22 mV (Fig. S2C) indicated the weak electronegativity of ECDs. Moreover, the high lipophilic alkyl chain content endowed ECDs with favorable lipophilicity, promoting their migration to lipid rafts and triggering the lipid raft-mediated endocytosis.

Generally, the universal strategy to design nanomaterials for targeted subcellular imaging could be achieved by coupling specific targeting groups, while the targeting units of some organelles, such as ER, are still indefinite. Recent researches have demonstrated that the subcellular localization of nanoparticles went hand in hand with the endocytic pathways closely [26]. As evidenced by the C1s band (Table S1) and 1H NMR characterization (Fig. S1), alkyl chains were successfully incorporated into CDs by using tridecanedioic acid as precursor. The cell membrane targeting ability of MCDs were attributed to the presence of long alkyl chains and carboxyl groups. The hydrophobic interaction of the aliphatic chains with lipid molecules made MCDs be anchored in plasma membrane, at the same time the low permeability of hydrophilic carboxyl groups prevented the internalization of CDs, resulting in the retention of MCDs in cell membrane [30,37]. XPS and 1H NMR analysis revealed that ECDs were characterized with higher content of alkyl chains and lower conjugation degree than MCDs. The increase in alkyl chain content endowed ECDs with enhanced lipophilicity. Meanwhile, the n-octanol alcohol/water partition coefficient value of ECDs (log P = 2.83) was much higher than that of MCDs (log P = 1.68). Consequently, the alkyl chains affected the targeting ability of ECDs greatly and the high-density alkyl chain would facilitate the internalization of CDs [38]. As ER with a hydrophobic membrane system is a workshop for lipid synthesis, thus the lipophilic alkyl chains of ECDs promoted the lipid-raft mediated endocytosis and delivered ECDs to the ER. Morusin is a kind of inducer that can effectively amplify oxidative stress of ER [39]. Fig. S8 illustrated the fluorescence images of ER morphology in the presence of morusin. It could be seen that the vesicle membrane formed by the expansion of ER was clearly labeled by ECDs, confirming the prospective interaction between ECDs and membrane of ER.

To verify the rationality of organelle-targeting ability of CDs, control experiment was performed using CCDs and ACDs. CCDs possessed a large number of carboxyl groups and no alkyl chains, while ACDs owned amount of alkyl groups but no carboxyl groups on the surface (Fig. S9A). After incubation with cells, it was found that only weak fluorescence scattered in the cytoplasm, rather than concentrated in organelle for CCDs, indicating that CCDs with abundant hydrophilic carboxyl groups were not prone to enter cells, and the lack of alkyl chains also didn’t allow its inserting into membrane structures such as cell membrane or ER membrane (Fig. S9B). On the contrary, ACDs could be successfully up-taken by cells and distributed in ER (Fig. S9C). These well demonstrated that the alkyl chain was indeed the key contributor to insert CDs into the membrane.

Long-term tracking and real-time monitoring are crucial for cell studying in biological process. Real-time imaging of MCF-7 cells in the presence of the two CDs were conducted under 2 h of continuous confocal scanning. As demonstrated in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity of DIL and ER-Tracker Red decreased more significantly than both CDs with the extension of irradiation time, suggesting that the superior anti-bleaching ability of these two CDs over the commercial probes.

It is reported that the labeling ability of membrane probes may be related to the state of the cell membrane, and the probes failed to target the cell membrane when the cells were unhealthy [40]. Accordingly, the cellular membrane retention ability of MCDs is the hinge of achieving long-range tracking. As cells naturally aged, their modality changed from normal to crumpled circle along with characteristic antennae [41]. The deterioration of the cell state leads to an increase in the permeability of the membrane, probes can readily traverse the cell membrane to enter the inner of the cells [40]. The cell aging was thus imaged with MCDs and commercial probe DIL. As shown in Fig. 4 A, DIL gradually diffused into the interior of cells with cell-aging, while MCDs still retained firmly in membrane, and no significant fluorescence was observed from MCDs at intracellular region. These well indicated the strong cell membrane anchoring ability of MCDs. Cisplatin is cytotoxic drug and commonly used to induce cell apoptosis [42,43]. Fig. 4B illustrated the imaging results of MCF-7 cells incubated with 25 mM cisplatin. As can be seen that with the process of culture time, the cell membrane became disorganized, gradually bubbling and swelling. The plasma membrane staining with MCDs clearly outlined the continuous dynamic changes of cell membranes, providing clear fluorescence images of membrane morphology in apoptotic period. This further demonstrated that MCDs were indeed perfectly trapped within the cell membrane, or else it would eventually be transported to the cytoplasm through endocytosis or diffusion [44]. Encouraged by the commendable retention ability of MCDs in cell membrane, its application in indicating cell passage number was explored. MCF-7 cells were stained by MCDs and DIL for 5 passages without repeated co-incubation, and the CLSM images were collected under the identical conditions. With the increase of culture passages number, the fluorescence intensity of MCDs and DIL gradually decreased (Fig. 4C). Compared with the gradual appearance of red fluorescence of DIL in cytoplasm, the blue fluorescence of MCDs still firmly insisted in cell membrane. We also captured the fluorescence behaviors of MCDs and DIL during cell division (Fig. 4D). MCDs clearly depicted the invagination of cell membrane, dividing the cytoplasm into two new cells, while DIL was distributed in the whole cytoplasm homogeneously.

The excellent membrane retention property of MCDs was benefited from the presence of long alkyl chains that embed them into the lipid bilayer of cell membrane. Meanwhile, the repulsion between carboxyl groups and the membrane was securely supposed to fulfil a role in suppressing the probes’ ability to penetrate the membrane [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of cholesterol-induced lipid order changes of biomembranes
ce:section.ce:para: The solvatochromism is expected for probes to perceive changes in lipid order of the plasma membrane, shifting the emission to long wavelengths from the polar dehydrated phase (Lo phase) to the polar hydrated phase (Ld phase) [46]. The fluorescence spectra of MCDs in different solvents showed that their emission maximum exhibited polarity-dependence, a red-shift of fluorescence emission occurred with increased polarity (Fig. S11A). As the surface of MCDs was negatively charged, the phenomena might be ascribed to the charged proximal polar groups of MCDs, which were easily susceptible to solvent environment [47,48]. The spectral differences between lipid composition in the phase state of cytomembrane is an important requirement for the development of lipid domain probes [49]. We subsequently manipulated large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs) with different phospholipids as models to simulate cell membranes. The unsaturated lipid DOPC manifested as the Ld phase under both basal and cholesterol-containing conditions (denoted as DOPC or DOPC/CL, respectively). The combination of sphingomyelin and cholesterol (SM/CL) or DPPC and cholesterol (DPPC/CL) was characterized as the Lo phase. With regard to MCDs, the emission maximum gradually blue-shifted following the membrane phases sequence of Ld → Lβ → Lo (see Fig. S11B and Table S2), consistent well with the reported results [50]. Moreover, the binding of MCDs with DOPC LUVs resulted in an approximately 100-fold increase in their fluorescence quantum yields. This dramatic difference might be related to the effective membrane binding of MCDs, which made MCDs an attractive probe for effective membrane staining without external assistance.

Since cholesterol is a key component of the Lo phase, in order to verify the ability of MCDs in distinguishing the presence of Lo phase and Ld phase in cell membrane, cholesterol depleting agent MβCD extraction was exploited to regulate cholesterol content in biofilms [51]. As illustrated Fig. S11C, the emission maximum of MCDs in MCF-7 cells located between that of Lo and Ld phases in the model membrane, which was highly consistent with the unsaturated lipids, SM and cholesterol contents in normal eukaryotic cell membranes [52]. As the duration of MβCD treatment prolonged, the cholesterol of cells was depleted, leading to the red-shift MCDs spectra, progressively closer to the emission of Ld vesicles (DOPC). Subsequently, in order to investigate the distribution changes of structural domains with different lipid orders (Lo phase/Ld phase) in cell membranes, the cells before and after MβCD treatment were attempted to visualize via confocal fluorescence microscopy. Due to the location of the emission of MCDs changing in pace with the phase state, fluorescence ratiometric imaging was performed by dividing their emission into blue and green regions (Fig. S11D). After the consumption of cholesterol by MβCD, significant changes in the pseudo color on the cell membrane was observed, revealing an increase in the fluorescence ratio (I green/I blue), which was in line with the presented band-shift in the fluorescence spectrum of cells (Fig. 5 ), indicating the transfer in contribution of emission of MCDs from blue to green. Though it is hard to achieve the direct observation of phase separation via optical microscope due to the limitations of imaging technology and resolution [53,54], the significant difference in fluorescence ratio of MCDs offered hope for the identification of lipid sequence in cell membranes, and also indicated the possibility of using MCDs to monitor the cholesterol content of the Lo phase in cell plasma membranes.

In conclusion, CDs with specific organelle imaging ability were rationally designed and constructed using m-diethylaminophenol and tridecanedioic acid as precursors. The condensation of m-diethylaminophenol catalyzed by tridecanedioic acid formed NR-like structure with strong affinity to lipid-rich organelles. The exquisite regulation on the feeding amount of tridecanedioic acid in CDs fabrication produced two CDs products with the ability to targeted-imaging of cell membrane and endoplasmic reticulum respectively. The moderate hydrophilic carboxyl groups and the long alkyl chains jointly prevent the internalization of CDs, making CDs firmly inserted into the plasma membrane. The high-density of alkyl chain accelerated the trend of CDs entering into cell interior, delivering lipophilic CDs to the endoplasmic reticulum. Benefit from the excellent membrane retention ability, membrane-targeted CDs demonstrated their potential in monitoring morphological change of cell membrane and long-term imaging. This work not only provides efficient probes for targeted-imaging of lipid-rich organelles, but also proposes a facile strategy to construct spectral probes for specific organelle imaging via modulating the surface property of CDs by exquisitely controlling fabrication conditions, and display a simple method to exploit specific and efficient trackers of different organelles as efficient platform to study the structure–property relationship concerning biological imaging probes.

Yanzhi Xing: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiaxin Li: Software, Formal analysis. Han Leng: Formal analysis. Yanfeng Zhang: Funding acquisition. Shuang E: Project administration. Jianhua Wang: Supervision, Resources. Xuwei Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the financial support from the Opening Project of Intelligent Policing Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (ZNJW2024KFZD005), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (N232410019) and Dalian Science and Technology Talent Innovation Support Project (2022RQ035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152068.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Liquid ordered and gel phases of lipid bilayers: Fluorescent probes reveal close fluidity but different hydration
